one_line_takeaway: ACE-Safety turns LLM jailbreak attack and defense into a single co-evolving loop — a group-aware strategy-guided MCTS attacker plus a tree-aware curriculum-RL trainer — yielding both a stronger attacker (higher ASR, fewer attempts) and a more robust defender (average ASR-LR 8.8% vs 65.1% undefended) than prior isolated methods.
key_finding: As an ATTACK, ACE-SafetyAttack gets the highest ASR with the fewest attempts across all three backbones both in-distribution (MergedHarm) and OOD (Malicious-Instruct) — e.g. 84.7% ASR / 8.3 avg attempts on Llama3-8B vs. the next-best ProAdvPrompter at 82.8% / 9.3. As a DEFENSE, ACE-SafetyDefense cuts average ASR-LR to 8.8% (from 65.1% no-defense) against GCG/PAIR/TAP across four models, beating every baseline including the closest red-teaming method MART (19.0%) and SafeDecoding (11.9%). It also holds up against unseen attacks (OOD strategy set OST and strongest-attack SAT settings) while incurring the smallest over-refusal increase and least helpfulness loss among defenses. Ablations show removing GS-MCTS (random rewrites), the prior-probability term, the tree-aware normalization, or the asymmetric/hard curriculum sets each raises ASR-LR, confirming every component contributes.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is ACE-Safety, a closed-loop framework that co-evolves a jailbreak ATTACK model (M_A) and a safety DEFENSE model (M_D) against each other, refereed by a training-free judge model (J_A). It has two novel components. (1) GS-MCTS (Group-aware Strategy-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search): the attack side searches over a fixed action space of K jailbreak rewriting strategies using a PUCT tree search whose prior P(s,a) blends the attacker's own generation log-probability with the defender's non-rejection likelihood, and evaluates each node on a GROUP of G sampled rewrites (reward = max harmfulness score) to damp LLM sampling randomness. (2) AC-TGPO (Adversarial Curriculum Tree-aware Group Policy Optimization): a GRPO-style RL trainer that adds a second, tree-level advantage normalization (weight W_j from node depth, discounted path value, strategy effectiveness) on top of standard group normalization, and feeds it an adversarial curriculum of Normal / Asymmetric (newest attacker vs. previous-round weaker defender) / Hard (previously-unsolved) sample sets across 4 co-evolution rounds. Distinguish from cited work: GCG/PAIR/TAP/AutoDAN/AdvPrompter/ProAdvPrompter/MPA are attack BASELINES it compares against, and ICAG/MART are the closest prior adversarial-game/red-teaming frameworks it argues it beats — they are not this paper's method.
threat_model: Jailbreaking of safety-aligned LLMs: an adversary crafts rewritten prompts (via a library of semantic strategies such as role-play, vague expression, scenario nesting) to bypass the target model's safety guardrails and elicit harmful content. The paper treats this as a two-sided arms race in which BOTH the attacker and defender are LLMs that update against each other, rather than a static attack or static defense. Notably the attack's GS-MCTS prior uses the defense model's token-level rejection log-probabilities (P_D = 1 - log p_D(w0 in rejection set)), so the attacker assumes at least grey/white-box access to the defender's output distribution during search; the trained attacker is then also evaluated black-box against fixed victim models (Vicuna, Llama3, Mistral).
limitations: Author-stated: evaluation is confined to public benchmarks and widely-studied open models, so nuanced or culturally-diverse adversarial scenarios and resource-constrained settings are untested; and they flag the dual-use ethics risk of publishing attack strategy knowledge. Reviewer-added: (1) the attack's prior probability needs the defender's rejection-token log-probs, a grey/white-box assumption that weakens the 'black-box jailbreak' framing; (2) the entire pipeline's rewards and the ASR-L metric hinge on the training-free LLM judge J_A, whose reliability is not independently validated; (3) attacker and defender share the same backbone and the co-evolution is only run for 4 rounds, leaving open whether gains hold against genuinely independent or stronger adversaries; (4) heavy compute (8x H800, MCTS + multi-round RL); (5) tested only up to 13B open models — no frontier or proprietary targets.

datasets_used: MergedHarm (training/main eval, 8:2 split; merges HarmBench + AdvBench + harmful subset of Toxic-Chat)
WildTeaming long-tailed OOD strategies (Jiang et al. 2024) used for the OST robustness setting
quantitative_results: Attack ASR-LR / avg attempts (ANA) on MergedHarm: ACE-SafetyAttack 91.4%/7.7 (Vicuna-13B), 84.7%/8.3 (Llama3-8B), 92.3%/6.4 (Mistral-7B-0.3)
Attack ASR-LR / ANA on OOD Malicious-Instruct: ACE-SafetyAttack 95.2%/7.4 (Vicuna-13B), 77.8%/14.8 (Llama3-8B), 89.6%/9.3 (Mistral-7B-0.3)
Best attack baseline ProAdvPrompter on Llama3-8B MergedHarm: 82.8%/9.3 (vs ACE 84.7%/8.3)
Defense avg ASR-LR across 4 models x GCG/PAIR/TAP: ACE-SafetyDefense 8.8% (best) vs No-Defense 65.1%, MART 19.0%, SafeDecoding 11.9%, CircuitBreakers 12.4%, SmoothLLM 20.5%, PPL 42.5%
Defense robustness to unseen attacks on Llama3-8B (ASR-LR, lower better): ACE OST 10.8% / SAT 11.8% vs Vanilla 84.7%/82.8% and SafeDecoding 15.4%/17.2%
ACE-SafetyDefense is the only method exceeding a 0.8 responsibility score on Llama3-8B (Appendix Fig. 9)
baselines_compared: Attack baselines: GCG, AutoDAN, GPTFuzzer, PAIR, TAP, AdvPrompter, ProAdvPrompter, MPA
Defense baselines (system-level): PPL, Self-reminder, SmoothLLM, ICAG
Defense baselines (fine-tuning/repr.-eng.): CircuitBreakers, LAT, CAT, R2D2, SafeDecoding, MART
